speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 2, interview with Erna Florsheim, October 20, 1996. I'd like to talk a little bit more about your town, Konigsbach. Did you have any-- any friends at all whom you played with, either earlier in your childhood or afterwards? Any-- any Jewish friends or any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one Jewish friend. Her name is Margot Rothschild. Her maiden name was Myer. And we played together, until she moved away from Konigsbach. Other than that, I did not have any friends. The-- most Jewish younger people were a little older than I, and moved away or emigrated and were away working. And the non-Jewish friends, I did not have any, because they were afraid to play with us, because there were a lot of people who were watching. And they were afraid. Maybe they would have wanted to. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Margot live near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did. She lived, oh, not even five minutes away from me. And I remember going to her house. And she had a swing. And we used to play there. And her mother used to come out to see that we are OK. And they had a-- a little garden. And that garden connected us with her uncle's house and garden. So we used to play there. And-- because she had no friends her-- her age. She is just a couple years older than I am. But we were closest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your 13th birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing that I remember is my mother baked me a special cake.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a plain pound cake, butter and eggs and so on. It was-- that was my favorite. As a matter of fact, I bake it quite often now. And I remember the way she used to do it. I used to sit with her in the kitchen and watch her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you bat mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In those days, you did not have that. And besides, our synagogue was-- did not exist any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me about your synagogue when it did exist, early in your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a two-story building. It-- the women were upstairs, separated from the men's section, who was downstairs. It was not too large. But it was very pretty, very nice. And we used to-- there was a small area downstairs where we could-- children-- where we could play. And-- but my mother didn't like me to run around there to play. She wanted me in the synagogue with her to pray, and she was very strict about that. But there were times when we used to go there. But as I said before, there were very few children there, I-- because it was a big Jewish community many years before. But I don't remember those times.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time when you were a child going to that synagogue, about how many people were there who were going? Not many. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe 20, 25. It-- it was not packed anymore as I understand it was years ago. As I said, I grew up in a time-- abnormal times, and where nothing was the way it used to be. Can you tell me about the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. He was a cantor. He was-- he officiated at funerals. Weddings, there weren't any. Bar mitzvahs, there weren't any. And the last bar mitzvah was my brother Julius. And I remember that. But he-- my family came from out of town who lived in Pforzheim. And there were very few people. The people from Konigsbach came. And there was just at home a little bit of a celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any non-Jewish people come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you tell me a little about holidays in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was always very special, because, as I said, my mother was very religious. And a holiday, it was so beautiful. And we had a dining area next to the kitchen. And that was set up with white tablecloth, the best china. And it was so festive. And even though we had no luxuries and we just had-- we had food. We never went hungry. We had potatoes. We had vegetables and fruits, and very little meat. But we were-- it was nice. We were sitting around the table. For example, on a Friday night, on the Shabbat, we were singing [NON-ENGLISH]. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] my mother was always very-- [SOBBING] about an hour before the Sabbath, she was-- she was so busy. She was-- that everything is in place and everything is just so. And we all got dressed for-- and we had our-- we had special clothes for Shabbat and our shoes for Shabbat and for Yontif. They were separate, not what we wore during the week. And it was very nice and these are nice memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing one of the [NON-ENGLISH] that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] That's one of them. And-- and of course, we-- we were- [NON-ENGLISH] we were singing before benching. And it was-- it was nice. It was-- we were always looking forward for Friday night or a holiday, whatever came. And I do remember on a Saturday night when my father came home from synagogue and he made havdalah. And I never liked that, because when the light went out from the havdalah candle, it-- the whole atmosphere changed. It was so different. It was like the everyday and I liked the Shabbat and the holidays so much, because it was so warm and comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that led to your uncle and aunt coming to live in the same house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe-- I believe they lived there before I was born already there. It was I guess just part of a family house. There were no special circumstances as far-- as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anyone else ever live in the same house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only after they left, as I said, one family, the non-Jewish family. They had I think one or two children. And I believe after 1938, Kristallnacht, I think they moved out. And soon after, my parents sold the house, because then they were trying to get ready to-- to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what circumstances led to the non-Jewish family living downstairs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. I was too young, you know, to-- to be involved in anything like this, or remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you know or think was happening in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew what was happening in the world, because everything was discussed at the table in our home about the Hitler time, about the Jews, about being-- about being prosecuted. And I remember after night, the Kristallnacht night 1938, a boy in the train that when I went to school said to me-- a non-Jewish boy said to me, you know that soon you will all have to leave. And that night I went home. I told my parents. So they said, well, how would he know? Apparently he must have heard from his parents what was in the works. And that day-- from that day on, I had a winter coat, a gray winter coat hanging in a closet. And I put handkerchiefs in there. I took things that meant a-- little things to me in readiness when that day comes that I would take that coat and take with me. And when it happened, I took that coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was with you that day when it happened? What did you have with you in the coat, and what else did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just very small items, let's say, a handkerchief. I had some candies in there. I had a very small doll that I just stuffed in there. Nothing of value, nothing of anything, but I wanted to have a warm coat with me for some reason. And I took that coat. I had a pair of gloves in there also I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it come to pass? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: One morning, [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] it is almost to the day, 56 years ago, October 22, 1940, the Gestapo came to the house and said, pack, but only take what you-- what you can carry, nothing more. And they put us-- which we did. And there was two other families there in Konigsbach. And we did that. They put us on a truck. We did not know where they would take us, what was going to happen. They-- and the people were standing there, the non-Jews. They just looked. And they were watching. No one applauded or anything like this that I remember. But they took us to Pforzheim. And there was a train waiting, where are we and all the Jews from-- that lived in Pforzheim were put on that train. And we had no idea what would be, where we would be taken, or anything. My uncle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my aunt and my young cousin-- she was two years old-- were also on that train. And my uncle took a Torah with him. And-- and there was another uncle that perished in Auschwitz, his daughter and his wife, they were also on that train, and a lot of people that we knew, that-- and I knew from school that I went with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- when we were on that train, the Gestapo with the guns and-- made announcements that all the money that people had on them should be thrown on the trucks out of the window. If they find anything, everyone will be shot on the spot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you should have seen the money that went on that truck, on those trucks, on that thing. I went through my-- I was so petrified. I went through my parents' pockets that they wouldn't have a nickel on them. So everything what I had I threw out the window, and so did all the other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- after a couple days, we landed in Gurs camp, the Gurs in France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us off a train. They took us then by truck to that-- to the camps. And all we saw was barracks, I don't know how many. They separated men and women. I stayed with my mother. My father was in a different area. And the mud was unbelievable. I had one pair of shoes on, which stuck in the mud. And after that, I wore what-- whoever gave me something, whatever I could. And I don't know what it is, but sometimes the little miracles do happen. And we-- they took us in a-- in a barrack. And there were some Gypsies. And I guess some of them were very handy. They had some woods and thing. And there was only the cement floor, you know. There were no beds or anything. There was a little bit of straw here and there. But one man came in. My mother and I, we were standing inside that door. And that-- one of the Gypsies, the men, gave my mother one-- a a bed. It was not a bed. It was just something off the floor, with some-- some wood nailed together. At least she didn't have to lay on the cement floor. And so how this just happened and how-- no one knows or can explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in that camp. And at night, we were so hungry we couldn't sleep. And we were watching the mice and the rats going up and down the poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to get one loaf of bread for I don't remember how many people were in that one barrack. But it was-- they had a piece of string. And it was measured. The bread, the loaf of bread was measured. And it was then each one got one slice of bread in 24 hours. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if God forbid somebody got a little bit more, it was unbelievable how people-- how angry people became. We got-- they gave us some hot soup. It was no soup. It was just water with something floating in there. I don't know. Some-- sometimes it was some tomatoes or something like this. This is-- and black coffee we got in the morning. And that's what we lived on. There-- the hygienic-- the facilities were unbelievable. I mean, we had-- on the outside, it was cold-- some cold water running certain times, so we washed ourselves as good as we could. The bathrooms were-- there were no bathrooms. There was just holes outside somewhere. And-- and it was cold. It was November already. It was near-- it was the Pyrenees. And it was cold and it was raining. And we had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we washed ourselves as good as we could. And of course, after a while, we had-- we were-- had lice from top to bottom. And once one of those Gypsies came and had like a bottle of some kind of a medication for against lice. My mother gave her piece of bread away in order to get that so she and I could be clean too. And we washed ourselves outside in cold water in the cold as good as we could. This-- and this is how we lived until they took us to a different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back and ask you a few questions before we go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The train that took you from Pforzheim to Camp Gurs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me was it very crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. It was-- it was packed. We were not in cattle cars. We were in regular train cars. But there-- every seat was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to sit?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to sit, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to stay with one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to stay with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you actually take with you when you left Konigsbach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall too much. We had-- my mother took some underwear for each of us, and I believe some sweaters and a skirt and some socks or stockings or something like that. And that's all. That's all we could take. That's all we could carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take any religious items?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother took, yes, a prayer book, the daily prayer book for the holidays, so we could not carry-- we left everything behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just left it. Did you hide anything, or did you try to give anything away for safe-keeping in case you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no time for anything. They-- we were surprised that morning. And we just packed and we-- we had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to eat or sleep on the train or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Food we didn't have, just what we had on us. My mother took a little food along, which it was not very much. But I-- we were not really hungry. We-- we were all exhausted most of the time. But sleep we could not. So it was-- we just were unsure and uncertain where they would take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there-- were there any toilet facilities on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Of course, it was-- for so many people it was very inadequate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How did you spend your time during that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just sat. We just sat. We were numb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to look out the window and appreciate a countryside you have never seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. It was too--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to the camp, did you manage to see your father afterwards, after you were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to have special passes to go. My mother was not too well, so I used to go by myself. And all around was barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [SOBBING] I remember saying to my father [CRYING] once, I wish I could play outside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was very-- how shall I say?-- very upset, because he couldn't help me. And he said, [SOBBING] the time will come when you can play outside. [CRYING] When you were there, was there any possibility of knowing how your brothers and your sister were? Was there any communication with the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not for a long time. One day we received a letter from my uncle that used to live at-- in Belgium but in the meantime went to Canada. And we heard through him that my brother-- my brothers and my sister are fine. And he relayed to his daughter in England, the one-- his daughter who sent the papers for my brother and sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He relayed the message that where we are and that we are alive. And they tried to send us money, my sister and brother. But once-- I believe once or twice we got a little something. My parents got something. But it was very little. They couldn't-- wouldn't let the gifts go through. And a lot of people who had relatives on the out-- in various countries who tried to help, but they never could. Or sometimes it did go-- rarely did it go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father spending his days while you were in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just sitting around. There was nothing to do. There was-- the hunger was there. He was-- he was tall. He was quite a strong person. And you could-- you could see every time from time to time when I saw him how he lost weight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People were just sitting around not doing anything. There was nothing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother try to teach you or school you in anyway during those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, she did. And we-- like she-- my mother and a former teacher that I had in Pforzheim, they used to get a few children of us together and we did arithmetic. We did spelling and things like this. Yes, we did have that. And we had everyday for a while, we studied with-- whichever way we could. And my mother and my-- my teacher-- her name was David, D-A-V-I-D, Fraulein David. She-- I forgot her first name. But she-- she did what she could for us, to help us, so we would not completely, you know, lack of education. She did the best she could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend Margot from Konigsbach, was she also there or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was.

speaker: interviewee
text: With her parents. She was also there with her parents. And she went later on to-- but that was much later-- to an uncle and aunt that lived in Paris. She went, but that was much later. She also was in Gurs with me and in Rivesaltes. And from there, she was in a-- they took her to a different children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were together in Gurs, were you able to enjoy one another's company in any way or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No not really, because she was in a different part of the camp. It was the same camp, but still a different section. And we saw each other occasionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: This little bed that the Gypsies gave your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep in the bed with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did sometimes, yes. When it was very cold, and my mother said-- we took our coats that we had with us and we covered ourselves. And yes I did sometimes. It was very narrow, but my mother was a very tiny woman. And she-- so-- and we-- we didn't-- we could share the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the Gypsies living in the same room with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. Later they did. At that time, only the ones that came from Germany-- we lived-- you know, from different parts of Germany. We met people that were from Karlsruhe, from Mannheim, and from all over. They all, you know, brought them to that train to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Gypsies treated any differently than the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what became of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become close with any of them? Were there any Gypsy children whom you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any friends there or any-- have any kind of close [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] involvements or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your relationship with your mother change during those times?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If anything, we became closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately a year and a half. And then they sent us to a different camp that was called Rivesaltes. And that was a different part. It was near the Spanish border. And there, we had a lot of the Spanish people. There was a revolution going on at the time. They came across to France. And that was near Perpignon. And-- but as they caught the people, they also put them into the camp. And they were with us the same barracks there. There it was very dry. And there was no mud. At least that was somehow an improvement, if nothing else. And my mother got very sick there, she had-- they gave us injections. I don't know what it was for. It was right into the bosom. And it was very painful. And my mother got an infection. They took her to some kind of a hospital or something. She was very sick for a long time. It cleared up eventually. My father got very sick, had infection with-- gum infections. They took him to Perpignon, to the city that was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that was unusual. But he was there. One day I wanted to visit him and I did have a permit. But because I was under age, they took me to prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue that on the next tape.
